stem: Let \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \), where \( x = [[m]]t^{2} + ([[p]]) \) and \( y = ([[n]])t + ([[q]]) \). Using the chain rule, what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at \( t = [[t0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( \frac{dz}{dt} = z_{x}\frac{dx}{dt} + z_{y}\frac{dy}{dt} \) with \( z_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), \( z_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \), \( \frac{dx}{dt} = 2([[m]])t \), \( \frac{dy}{dt} = ([[n]]) \). At \( t = [[t0]] \), \( x = [[xt]] \) and \( y = [[yt]] \), giving \( \frac{dz}{dt} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( \frac{dx}{dt} = [[m]]t \), forgetting the factor of 2 from differentiating \( [[m]]t^{2} \).
This adds the partial derivatives without multiplying by the path derivatives.
This swaps \( \frac{dx}{dt} \) and \( \frac{dy}{dt} \) between the two terms.
This uses only the \( x \)-term of the chain rule and drops the \( y \)-term.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( w = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} \), where \( x = [[m1]]t + ([[p1]]) \), \( y = [[m2]]t + ([[p2]]) \), and \( z = [[m3]]t + ([[p3]]) \). Using the (three-variable) chain rule, what is \( \dfrac{dw}{dt} \) at \( t = [[t0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule for a function of three variables, \( \frac{dw}{dt} = w_{x}\frac{dx}{dt} + w_{y}\frac{dy}{dt} + w_{z}\frac{dz}{dt} \), with \( w_{x} = 2([[a]])x \), \( w_{y} = 2([[b]])y \), \( w_{z} = 2([[c]])z \), and \( \frac{dx}{dt} = [[m1]] \), \( \frac{dy}{dt} = [[m2]] \), \( \frac{dz}{dt} = [[m3]] \). At \( t = [[t0]] \), \( x = [[xt]] \), \( y = [[yt]] \), \( z = [[zt]] \), giving \( \frac{dw}{dt} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating each squared term.
This drops the \( z \)-term of the chain rule entirely.
This forgets to multiply each partial derivative by the corresponding path derivative.
This cyclically shifts which path derivative multiplies which partial derivative.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \), and at a certain instant \( x = [[x0]] \), \( y = [[y0]] \), with \( \dfrac{dx}{dt} = [[dxdt]] \) and \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} = [[dydt]] \). Using the chain rule, what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at that instant?
solution: By the chain rule, \( \frac{dz}{dt} = z_{x}\frac{dx}{dt} + z_{y}\frac{dy}{dt} \), with \( z_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \) and \( z_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \) evaluated at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \). Substituting the given rates, \( \frac{dz}{dt} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps \( \frac{dx}{dt} \) and \( \frac{dy}{dt} \) between the two terms.
This adds the partial derivatives without multiplying by the given rates.
This uses only the \( x \)-term of the chain rule and drops the \( y \)-term.
This drops the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating each squared term.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: A point moves along a curve lying on the ellipsoid \( [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + [[c]]z^{2} = [[k]] \), which implicitly defines \( z \) as a function of \( x \) and \( y \) (for \( z > 0 \)). At a certain instant, \( x = [[x0]] \), \( y = [[y0]] \), \( z = [[z0]] \), and \( \dfrac{dx}{dt} = [[m]] \), \( \dfrac{dy}{dt} = [[n]] \). Using implicit differentiation together with the chain rule, what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at that instant?
solution: Differentiating \( [[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y^{2}+[[c]]z^{2}=[[k]] \) implicitly gives \( z_{x} = -\dfrac{[[a]]x}{[[c]]z} \) and \( z_{y} = -\dfrac{[[b]]y}{[[c]]z} \). By the chain rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = z_{x}\dfrac{dx}{dt} + z_{y}\dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{[[a]]([[x0]])([[m]]) + [[b]]([[y0]])([[n]])}{[[c]]([[z0]])} = [[answer]]\).
distractor_rationales: This drops the negative sign that comes from implicit differentiation.
This swaps \( \frac{dx}{dt} \) and \( \frac{dy}{dt} \) between the two terms.
This forgets to divide by \( [[c]]z \) at all.
This drops the \( y \)-term of the chain rule entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y^{2} + ([[c]])xy \), where \( y \) itself depends on \( x \) via \( y = [[m]]x^{2} + ([[p]]) \). Using the chain rule, what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dx} \) at \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dx} = z_{x} + z_{y}\dfrac{dy}{dx} \), since \( x \) is its own parameter (\( dx/dx=1 \)). Here \( z_{x} = 2([[a]])x + ([[c]])y \), \( z_{y} = 2([[b]])y + ([[c]])x \), and \( \dfrac{dy}{dx} = 2([[m]])x \). At \( x=[[x0]] \), \( y=[[y0]] \), giving \( \dfrac{dz}{dx} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( z_{y}\,dy/dx \) term entirely, treating \( z \) as if it depended only on \( x \) explicitly.
This drops the direct \( z_{x} \) term, keeping only the indirect dependence through \( y \).
This swaps which term gets multiplied by \( dy/dx \).
This drops the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating \( y=[[m]]x^{2}+[[p]] \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( z = xy \), where \( x = [[m]]t + ([[p]]) \) and \( y = [[n]]t + ([[q]]) \). Using the chain rule together with the product rule (since \( z_{x}=y \) and \( z_{y}=x \)), what is \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} \) at \( t = [[t0]] \)?
solution: By the product rule, \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = \dfrac{d(xy)}{dt} = y\dfrac{dx}{dt} + x\dfrac{dy}{dt} \). At \( t=[[t0]] \), \( x=[[xt]] \), \( y=[[yt]] \), \( \dfrac{dx}{dt}=[[m]] \), \( \dfrac{dy}{dt}=[[n]] \), giving \( \dfrac{dz}{dt} = [[yt]]([[m]]) + [[xt]]([[n]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This swaps which path derivative multiplies which variable, using \( x\,dx/dt+y\,dy/dt \) instead of \( y\,dx/dt+x\,dy/dt \).
This drops the \( x\,dy/dt \) term entirely, keeping only \( y\,dx/dt \).
This multiplies \( x \) and \( y \) directly instead of applying the product rule for \( dz/dt \).
This multiplies the two path rates together instead of applying the product rule.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
